findings.summary: Piperine, the principal alkaloid of black pepper, is a well-characterised inhibitor of CYP2C19, the enzyme primarily responsible for omeprazole metabolism; multiple pharmacokinetic studies confirm piperine co-administration increases omeprazole AUC by 30–65% depending on CYP2C19 genotype, with the largest effect in extensive metabolisers.
findings.citation: Bhardwaj et al., Eur J Clin Pharmacol 2002

findings.summary: Although the piperine-omeprazole interaction enhances acid-suppression efficacy, elevated omeprazole exposure increases the long-term risks associated with high-dose PPI use: hypomagnesaemia, Clostridioides difficile infection, and reduced calcium absorption leading to fracture risk in older patients.
findings.citation: Lam et al., Gastroenterology 2013; FDA PPI safety communications

findings.summary: BioPerine (standardised 95% piperine) is actively marketed as a bioavailability enhancer and is widely added to supplement formulations; patients who take supplement stacks containing piperine while also on omeprazole may be unaware of the pharmacokinetic interaction.
findings.citation: Shoba et al., Planta Med 1998 (piperine bioavailability enhancement review)
